ideas_currently_on_table: Identifying active sites via operando TEM
chunk_summaries: Emily proposed using city sensor networks for CO2 measurement and Alissa raised the possibility of extending this to indoor air capture; Emily drove most of the ideation while Alissa asked clarifying questions. The tone was collaborative with visible head nods and active backchannels. The group ended the chunk in early divergent exploration with no specific project named.

The group continued to explore the effects of temperature on electrochemical systems, with Marcel and Feng Jiao sharing detailed knowledge about high-temperature mechanisms and solid oxide electrolyzers. Oana highlighted a methodological gap in studying gas-phase mechanisms at high temperatures and explicitly suggested Will had the unique expertise to address it, prompting a brief commitment signal. Carlos bridged the discussion by comparing kinetic measurement techniques in heterogeneous thermal catalysis to the mass transport challenges in electrocatalysis. The conversation remained highly divergent and exploratory, focusing on methodological challenges without converging on a specific joint project.

The group engaged in a highly technical discussion about the definition and utility of overpotentials, with Marcel challenging its usefulness and Oana and Gary defending it. Jenny Yang actively synthesized the conversation in a shared Google Doc, steering the group toward identifying active sites and bridging the gap between experimental spectroscopy and computational modeling. Will Bowman introduced operando TEM as a potential method, though he noted its limitations, keeping the conversation in a divergent, exploratory phase regarding methodological challenges.

In the final chunk of the session, Roger Aines structured a round-robin closing, inviting final thoughts from all participants. The group synthesized their earlier discussions, with Feng Lin and Carlos Morales Guio reiterating the methodological challenges of operando measurements and mass transport, while Yuanyue Liu explicitly highlighted the need for computational and experimental scientists to collaborate. The session ended on a positive, convergent note, with participants identifying shared interests but without committing to a specific joint project.
